graph_vector_fulltext.message:
I don’t have enough information in the provided context to define what constitutes a “serious incident” (the definition text isn’t included in the excerpts), nor to describe the post‑market monitoring reporting flow for serious incidents.

What the context does show about post‑market monitoring and governance is:
- Post‑market monitoring is supported by “information” (the document states that information “facilitates post market monitoring”).
- For general‑purpose AI models (GPAI), monitoring and enforcement is at Union level: the Commission has competence for enforcement/supervision of obligations on providers of GPAI models, and the AI Office monitors compliance and implementation as regards GPAI models.
- The AI Office can investigate possible infringements of the rules on providers of GPAI models (on its own initiative, following monitoring, or upon request from market surveillance authorities), and it can request documentation/information and request measures from GPAI model providers.
- Market surveillance authorities (national) can request assistance from the AI Office and can request an investigation by the AI Office; they may be unable to access certain information related to the general‑purpose AI model.
- Downstream providers can lodge complaints to the AI Office about possible infringements of the rules on providers of GPAI models and systems.
- A scientific panel (independent experts) supports the AI Office’s monitoring activities, provides “qualified alerts,” and reports on general‑purpose AI models (and notifies the AI Office).

On “who monitors / who receives reports” (based strictly on the context):
- Monitors (GPAI): AI Office (Union level) monitors compliance with obligations for providers of GPAI models and investigates possible infringements; it consults the Scientific Panel and may use independent experts for evaluations.
- Receives inputs/complaints (GPAI): AI Office receives complaints from downstream providers; it can also be requested to investigate by market surveillance authorities; it can receive “qualified alerts” via the scientific panel notifying it.
- National market surveillance authorities: investigate AI systems (including high‑risk AI systems) and can cooperate with and seek assistance from the AI Office; they can inform other market surveillance authorities and the Board.

How this differs for AI systems built on GPAI models (from the context):
- AI systems may be “built on” general‑purpose AI models, but the governance emphasis differs: market surveillance authorities investigate AI systems, while the AI Office/Commission specifically monitor and enforce obligations for providers of the underlying general‑purpose AI models—and can step in especially where authorities cannot access certain model information.
- The AI Office’s role is particularly tied to the GPAI layer (provider obligations, access to model documentation/information, evaluations, and requesting measures), whereas market surveillance authorities are positioned to act on the downstream AI system in the market.

If you can share the specific EU AI Act article/recital text you’re using (or the relevant excerpt defining “serious incident” and the reporting obligation), I can map it precisely to: (1) the “serious incident” definition, (2) the post‑market monitoring trigger/requirements, and (3) the full reporting chain (provider → deployer? → national authority? → AI Office/Commission) including differences for GPAI-based systems.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: Define what constitutes a 'serious incident', connect it to the post-market monitoring obligations, and trace the governance chain: who monitors, who receives reports, and how does this differ for AI systems built on general-purpose AI models?

What the context does show about post‑market monitoring and governance is:
- Post‑market monitoring is supported by “information” (the document states that information “facilitates post market monitoring”).
- For general‑purpose AI models (GPAI), monitoring and enforcement is at Union level: the Commission has competence for enforcement/supervision of obligations on providers of GPAI models, and the AI Office monitors compliance and implementation as regards GPAI models.
- The AI Office can investigate possible infringements of the rules on providers of GPAI models (on its own initiative, following monitoring, or upon request from market surveillance authorities), and it can request documentation/information and request measures from GPAI model providers.
- Market surveillance authorities (national) can request assistance from the AI Office and can request an investigation by the AI Office; they may be unable to access certain information related to the general‑purpose AI model.
- Downstream providers can lodge complaints to the AI Office about possible infringements of the rules on providers of GPAI models and systems.
- A scientific panel (independent experts) supports the AI Office’s monitoring activities, provides “qualified alerts,” and reports on general‑purpose AI models (and notifies the AI Office).

On “who monitors / who receives reports” (based strictly on the context):
- Monitors (GPAI): AI Office (Union level) monitors compliance with obligations for providers of GPAI models and investigates possible infringements; it consults the Scientific Panel and may use independent experts for evaluations.
- Receives inputs/complaints (GPAI): AI Office receives complaints from downstream providers; it can also be requested to investigate by market surveillance authorities; it can receive “qualified alerts” via the scientific panel notifying it.
- National market surveillance authorities: investigate AI systems (including high‑risk AI systems) and can cooperate with and seek assistance from the AI Office; they can inform other market surveillance authorities and the Board.

How this differs for AI systems built on GPAI models (from the context):
- AI systems may be “built on” general‑purpose AI models, but the governance emphasis differs: market surveillance authorities investigate AI systems, while the AI Office/Commission specifically monitor and enforce obligations for providers of the underlying general‑purpose AI models—and can step in especially where authorities cannot access certain model information.
- The AI Office’s role is particularly tied to the GPAI layer (provider obligations, access to model documentation/information, evaluations, and requesting measures), whereas market surveillance authorities are positioned to act on the downstream AI system in the market.

If you can share the specific EU AI Act article/recital text you’re using (or the relevant excerpt defining “serious incident” and the reporting obligation), I can map it precisely to: (1) the “serious incident” definition, (2) the post‑market monitoring trigger/requirements, and (3) the full reporting chain (provider → deployer? → national authority? → AI Office/Commission) including differences for GPAI-based systems.

Content: Text Content:
 the obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models should be a competence of the  Commission. The AI Office should be able to carry out all necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation  of this Regulation as regards general-purpose AI models. It should be able to investigate possible infringements of  the rules on providers of general-purpose AI models both on its own initiative, following the results of its  monitoring activities, or upon request from market surveillance authorities in line with the conditions set out in this
----
 certain situations those  operators could act in more than one role at the same time and should therefore fulfil cumulatively all relevant  obligations associated with those roles. For example, an operator could act as a distributor and an importer at the  same time. (84) To ensure legal certainty, it is necessary to clarify that, under certain specific conditions, any distributor, importer,  deployer or other third-party should be considered to be a provider of a high-risk AI system
----
 should be able to take the necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of and  compliance with the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models laid down in this Regulation. The AI  Office should be able to investigate possible infringements in accordance with the powers provided for in this  Regulation, including by requesting documentation and information, by conducting evaluations, as well as by  requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. When conducting evaluations, in order to make  use of independent expertise, the
----
  monitoring activities, or upon request from market surveillance authorities in line with the conditions set out in this  Regulation. To support effective monitoring of the AI Office, it should provide for the possibility that downstream  providers lodge complaints about possible infringements of the rules on providers of general-purpose AI models and  systems. (163) With a view to complementing the governance systems for general-purpose AI models, the scientific panel should  support the monitoring activities of the AI Office and may, in certain
----

chunks.text: certain situations those operators could act in more than one role at the same time and should therefore fulfil cumulatively all relevant obligations associated with those roles. For example, an operator could act as a distributor and an importer at the same time. (84) To ensure legal certainty, it is necessary to clarify that, under certain specific conditions, any distributor, importer, deployer or other third-party should be considered to be a provider of a high-risk AI system

the obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models should be a competence of the Commission. The AI Office should be able to carry out all necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of this Regulation as regards general-purpose AI models. It should be able to investigate possible infringements of the rules on providers of general-purpose AI models both on its own initiative, following the results of its monitoring activities, or upon request from market surveillance authorities in line with the conditions set out in this

should be able to take the necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of and compliance with the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models laid down in this Regulation. The AI Office should be able to investigate possible infringements in accordance with the powers provided for in this Regulation, including by requesting documentation and information, by conducting evaluations, as well as by requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. When conducting evaluations, in order to make use of independent expertise, the

monitoring activities, or upon request from market surveillance authorities in line with the conditions set out in this Regulation. To support effective monitoring of the AI Office, it should provide for the possibility that downstream providers lodge complaints about possible infringements of the rules on providers of general-purpose AI models and systems. (163) With a view to complementing the governance systems for general-purpose AI models, the scientific panel should support the monitoring activities of the AI Office and may, in certain
